title: 10 best herbal teas for gastritis
intro: Gastritis, or inflammation of the stomach lining, can cause discomfort and pain. Certain herbal teas may help soothe symptoms and promote healing. Chamomile tea is known for its anti-inflammatory properties, which may help reduce inflammation in the stomach. Licorice root tea, which contains deglycyrrhizinated licorice (DGL), can help increase the mucus coating of the stomach, protecting it from acid and irritants. Peppermint tea can also aid in digestion and alleviate symptoms of gastritis. However, it's important to consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new herbal remedies, especially for those with underlying health conditions or taking medications.

teas.tea_desc: Chamomile tea aids gastritis relief due to its anti-inflammatory properties, which reduce inflammation in the stomach lining. This tea's antispasmodic qualities alleviate stomach cramps and spasms associated with gastritis. Chamomile contains protective compounds, including flavonoids, that safeguard the stomach against damage from acid and bacteria. Furthermore, chamomile tea's soothing effects on the digestive system promote relaxation, helping to decrease stress-induced gastritis symptoms. Lastly, chamomile tea's mild bitter taste stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, facilitating better nutrient absorption and faster stomach emptying, ultimately benefiting gastritis symptoms.
tea_parts: Flowers: The chamomile flowers contain anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the stomach lining, making it effective for gastritis.
Leaves: Rich in flavonoids, chamomile leaves help decrease stomach inflammation, providing relief from gastritis symptoms.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds are known for their carminative properties, which aid in relieving gastritis-related bloating and dispelling gas.
tea_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water in a saucepan for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon of dried chamomile flowers to a tea infuser and place in a cup.
Pour the boiled water over the chamomile flowers and let steep for 5-10 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser and discard the chamomile flowers.
Drink the chamomile tea while warm, up to 3 times a day, after meals.
tea_constituents: Apigenin: A flavonoid that has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help reduce inflammation and pain in the stomach lining caused by gastritis.
Bisabolol: A terpene that has anti-inflammatory and anti-ulcer properties, which can help reduce inflammation and promote healing of the stomach lining in gastritis.
Chamazulene: A chemical compound that has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce inflammation and protect the stomach lining from damage caused by gastritis.
Matricin: A precursor to chamazulene that has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help reduce inflammation and pain in the stomach lining caused by gastritis.

Alpha-bisabolol oxides A and B: Oxides of bisabolol that have anti-inflammatory and wound-healing properties, which can help reduce inflammation and promote healing of the stomach lining in gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Licorice tea aids gastritis by soothing inflamed mucous membranes in the stomach, thanks to its active compound, glycyrrhizin. This compound generates a protective coating along the stomach lining, preventing hydrochloric acid from causing further damage. Moreover, licorice tea encourages the production of mucin, a vital component of the stomach's defensive mucosal barrier. As a result, the tea alleviates pain, decreases inflammation, and fosters the healing process for gastritis symptoms. Furthermore, licorice tea boasts antimicrobial properties, hindering the growth of H. pylori, a bacterium frequently responsible for gastritis.
tea_parts: Roots: Rich in glycyrrhizin, a compound with anti-inflammatory properties that helps soothe irritated gastric mucosa.
Rhizomes: Contain substances like triterpenoid saponins which protect the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis.
Leaves: Packed with flavonoids, offering antioxidant and anti-ulcer properties, helping in the treatment of gastritis.
Flowers: Contain essential oils and possess carminative properties, aiding in the expulsion of gas from the stomach, alleviating symptoms of gastritis.
Seeds: Loaded with glycosides and polysaccharides, they help in the management of gastritis by reducing inflammation and promoting mucosal healing.
tea_recipe: In a teapot, combine 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root and 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes to allow the active compounds to infuse.
Strain the tea to remove the licorice root material.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste, but avoid milk or sugar.
Consume the tea while it's still warm, ideally after meals, for gastritis relief.
tea_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound has an anti-inflammatory effect, which can help reduce gastric inflammation caused by gastritis.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants can help protect the stomach lining from damage and reduce inflammation.
Chalcones: These compounds have been shown to have gastroprotective effects, helping to prevent and heal gastritis.
Coumarins: These compounds have anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of gastritis.
Triterpenoids: These compounds have been shown to have gastroprotective effects, helping to prevent and heal gastritis.
Volatile oils: These compounds have carminative properties, which can help reduce bloating and gas in the stomach caused by gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Marshmallow tea, derived from the marshmallow plant's root, contains mucilage, a substance that turns into a gel when mixed with water. This gel coats the stomach lining, providing a protective barrier against acid and digestive juices. By forming this barrier, marshmallow tea alleviates gastritis symptoms, such as pain, inflammation, and irritation. Additionally, this soothing action encourages the healing of stomach ulcers and lesions caused by gastritis. Marshmallow tea's demulcent properties, combined with its anti-inflammatory effects, make it an ideal natural remedy for treating gastritis, offering quick relief and promoting overall gastric health.
tea_parts: Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which coats the stomach lining and helps protect it from acid and inflammation.
Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant have a high mucilage content, which helps soothe and protect the stomach lining from acid and inflammation.
Flowers: The flowers of the marshmallow plant contain flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce gastritis symptoms.
Seeds: The seeds of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which can help soothe and protect the stomach lining from acid and inflammation.
tea_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 2 cups of water, 1 tablespoon of dried marshmallow root, and 1 teaspoon of grated ginger; simmer for 15-20 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a teapot or mug, pressing on the solids to extract all liquid.
Discard the marshmallow root and ginger solids.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired.
Serve the marshmallow tea warm and enjoy up to 3 times daily for gastritis relief.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow tea coats the stomach, protecting it from irritants and reducing inflammation, making it helpful for gastritis.
Flavonoids: Marshmallow tea contains flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the stomach lining caused by gastritis.
Tannins: The tannins in marshmallow tea have astringent properties, helping to reduce stomach acidity, which can alleviate symptoms of gastritis.
Polysaccharides: The polysaccharides in marshmallow tea have a soothing effect on the gastrointestinal tract, helping to protect the stomach from damage and reduce the symptoms of gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Slippery elm tea provides relief for gastritis due to its demulcent properties, which involve the presence of mucilage that turns into a slick gel when in contact with water. This gel coats the lining of the stomach, protecting it from acidity and reducing inflammation. The tea's soothing effect on the gastrointestinal tract further alleviates pain and discomfort caused by gastritis. Moreover, slippery elm contains tannins known for their astringent qualities, which help contract and heal tissues, promoting the stomach's overall health and well-being. Lastly, this herbal tea boosts the production of mucus in the stomach, providing an added layer of protection and creating an unfavorable environment for acid buildup.
tea_parts: Bark: The bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make medicinal tea for gastritis due to its demulcent properties, which means it contains large amounts of mucilage that can soothe and protect the stomach lining.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves are used in herbal teas for gastritis because they contain tannins that have astringent properties, helping to reduce inflammation and promote healing in the stomach.

Inner Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm tree is the most commonly used part for making medicinal tea for gastritis. It is rich in antioxidants, nutrients, and mucilage, offering a protective layer to the stomach and facilitating quick recovery.

Seeds: Slippery elm seeds are also used to make tea for gastritis due to their high mucilage content, offering a soothing effect and providing relief from symptoms associated with gastritis, such as pain and inflammation.

Roots: Although not as commonly used as the bark and leaves, slippery elm roots have therapeutic properties that can help with gastritis symptoms. Rich in antioxidants and mucilage, roots are used in teas for their anti-inflammatory effects and ability to soothe the digestive tract.
tea_recipe: Measure - Using a measuring spoon, measure out 1-2 teaspoons of dried slippery elm bark.
Pour - Boil 8 ounces of water and pour it over the slippery elm bark in a cup.
Steep - Steep the mixture, covered, for 5-10 minutes to allow the properties to be released.
Strain - Strain the tea to remove the solid particles and ensure a smooth drink.
Serve - Sip the slippery elm tea tea while warm, up to three times a day for gastritis relief.
tea_constituents: Mucilage: Forms a protective barrier over ulcers and sores in the digestive tract, reducing irritation and promoting healing.
Tannins: Have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the stomach lining caused by gastritis.
Gallic acid: Possesses antibacterial properties, potentially helping to combat Helicobacter pylori, a bacteria commonly associated with gastritis.
Kaempferol: Exhibits antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, protecting the stomach lining from further damage and promoting recovery.
Catechins: Contribute to the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of slippery elm tea, aiding in soothing gastritis symptoms.

teas.tea_desc: Meadowsweet tea contains active compounds, such as salicylic acid and flavonoids, that boast anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties. These compounds work together to soothe irritation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis symptoms. Furthermore, meadowsweet tea promotes the production of gastric mucus, creating a protective barrier against stomach acids. Consequently, this natural remedy helps restore balance to the digestive system, alleviating gastritis-related discomfort.
tea_parts: Flowers: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, meadowsweet flowers help soothe gastritis-related irritation and promote stomach lining healing.
Leaves: High in tannins, meadowsweet leaves provide astringent properties that aid in reducing stomach inflammation and alleviating gastritis symptoms.
Rhyzomes: Containing salicylic acid, meadowsweet rhyzomes help relieve pain and reduce fever, offering comfort to individuals suffering from gastritis.
tea_recipe: Measure out 2 teaspoons of dried meadowsweet flowers.
Place the herbs in a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the meadowsweet.
Steep for 10 minutes to extract the medicinal compounds.
Strain the tea and drink it while warm, up to three times a day.
tea_constituents: Salicylic acid: This compound, also found in aspirin, has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce gastric inflammation associated with gastritis.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants can help protect the lining of the stomach, reducing the risk of damage from gastritis.
Tannins: These astringent compounds can help reduce inflammation and soothe irritation in the stomach, alleviating symptoms of gastritis.
Phenolic acids: These compounds have antioxidant properties that can help reduce free radical damage in the stomach, protecting against gastritis.
Essential oils: The essential oils in meadowsweet have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help alleviate symptoms of gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Catnip tea contains nepetalactone, a compound known to have antispasmodic and carminative properties. These properties make it effective in reducing inflammation in the stomach lining, a primary symptom of gastritis. Furthermore, it relaxes the gastrointestinal tract, easing digestion and reducing discomfort. Additionally, catnip tea's antioxidants aid in the elimination of harmful free radicals in the stomach, supporting overall gastric health. Its anti-inflammatory and soothing effects also alleviate pain and promote the healing of damaged tissues. Thus, the unique properties of catnip tea make it an ideal natural remedy for gastritis relief.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in anti-inflammatory properties, leaves of the catnip plant soothe gastritis-related inflammation.
Flowers: Containing calming compounds, catnip flowers help reduce gastritis-induced stomach spasms.
Stems: High in antioxidants, catnip stems aid in healing the stomach lining damaged by gastritis.
Roots: Known for their mucilage content, catnip roots create a protective coating on the stomach lining, beneficial for gastritis.
tea_recipe: Infuse one teaspoon of dried catnip leaves in boiling water for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, leaving only the liquid.
Allow the tea to cool down to a warm temperature.
Drink one cup of the catnip tea up to three times a day.
Continue consumption for several days until gastritis symptoms subside.
tea_constituents: Nepetalactone: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis.
Valepotriates: These compounds have anti-spasmodic properties that can help reduce muscle spasms in the gastrointestinal tract, providing relief from symptoms of gastritis.
Nepetalic acid: This compound has astringent properties that can help reduce gastric acid production, providing relief from gastritis symptoms.
Rosmarinic acid: This compound has antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining from damage caused by free radicals, reducing the risk of gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Fennel tea relieves gastritis by decreasing inflammation in the stomach lining. This herbal tea's anti-inflammatory properties stem from its active components, primarily anethole and fenchone. Fennel tea's soothing effect on the gastrointestinal tract also promotes the production of digestive enzymes, facilitating proper nutrient absorption and further alleviating gastritis symptoms. Additionally, this tea's carminative effects help reduce gastric spasms and expel excess gas, providing further relief from gastritis discomfort.
tea_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds contain volatile oils and antioxidants that aid in reducing inflammation and soothing the gastrointestinal tract, making them ideal for gastritis relief.
Leaves: The feathery leaves of fennel are rich in vitamins and minerals, possessing calming properties that help alleviate gastritis symptoms.
Rhizomes: Fennel rhizomes have a robust aroma and anti-inflammatory compounds, making them an excellent choice for soothing irritated stomach lining in gastritis sufferers.
tea_recipe: Chop one tablespoon of fresh fennel seeds or half a teaspoon of dried fennel seeds.
Place the chopped fennel seeds in a tea infuser or strainer.
Heat eight ounces of water to boiling, then pour it over the fennel seeds.
Let the tea steep for five to ten minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strainer and enjoy the fennel tea while it's still warm.
tea_constituents: Anethole: This compound has anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that can help alleviate pain and inflammation in the stomach lining caused by gastritis.
Fenchone: This compound has carminative properties, which can help reduce bloating and expel gas, providing relief from symptoms of gastritis.
Limonene: This compound has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation caused by gastritis.
Estragole: This compound has antispasmodic properties, which can help relieve abdominal pain and spasms caused by gastritis.
Menthol: This compound has antispasmodic and analgesic properties, which can help alleviate pain and discomfort in the stomach caused by gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Peppermint tea aids gastritis by calming the stomach muscles, which reduces the likelihood of spasms and discomfort. This tea contains menthol, a compound with antispasmodic properties that promotes the relaxation of the gastrointestinal tract. Furthermore, peppermint tea has carminative effects, helping to alleviate gas and bloating. Additionally, it boasts anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that soothe irritated gastric tissues, reducing associated pain. Lastly, peppermint tea enhances digestion, allowing for a quicker recovery from gastritis symptoms.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in menthol and menthone, peppermint leaves relieve gastritis symptoms due to their antispasmodic and analgesic properties.
Flowers: Containing volatile oils, peppermint flowers soothe inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis.
Seeds: Packed with antioxidants and therapeutic compounds, peppermint seeds aid in reducing gastritis-related inflammation and pain.
tea_recipe: In a small pot, boil 8 ounces of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves or 1 peppermint tea bag to the boiling water.
Reduce heat, let it simmer for 2-3 more minutes.
Remove from heat, cover and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea, discard the solids and enjoy.
tea_constituents: Menthol: Provides a cooling effect that can help to soothe and relax the gastrointestinal tract, reducing inflammation associated with gastritis.
Methyl salicylate: Has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce pain and inflammation in the stomach lining caused by gastritis.
Limonene: A compound with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can protect the stomach lining from damage caused by gastritis.
Vitamin A: Promotes the repair of stomach lining tissue, aiding in the healing process for individuals with gastritis.
Rosmarinic acid: A compound with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce inflammation and damage caused by gastritis.
Flavonoids: A group of compounds with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help to protect the stomach lining and promote healing in individuals with gastritis.

teas.tea_desc: Ginger tea brings immediate relief to gastritis symptoms due to its powerful anti-inflammatory properties, which reduce inflammation in the lining of the stomach. This soothing beverage stimulates the production of digestive juices, promoting a healthy digestive process and alleviating discomfort. Furthermore, ginger tea's natural antacid effects neutralize excess acid in the stomach, offering rapid symptomatic relief. Additionally, ginger tea's carminative properties aid in expelling gas, preventing bloating and additional pain. Ultimately, the regular consumption of ginger tea strengthens the mucosal lining of the stomach, enhancing its resilience against gastritis.
tea_parts: Rhizomes: Ginger rhizomes are widely used to make medicinal tea for gastritis due to their strong anti-inflammatory and soothing properties.
Leaves: Fresh ginger leaves contain high levels of antioxidants and volatile oils, providing relief from gastritis symptoms when consumed as a tea.
Roots: Ginger roots have potent digestive and anti-inflammatory properties, making them a popular choice for medicinal tea to treat gastritis.
Flowers: Ginger flowers contain essential oils and bioactive compounds, providing gastritis relief through regular consumption as a tea.
Seeds: Ginger seeds possess antiemetic, analgesic, and anti-inflammatory properties, aiding in the management of gastritis symptoms when brewed as a tea.
tea_recipe: Peel and thinly slice 1 inch of fresh ginger root.
Place ginger slices in a pot and add 4 cups of water.
Bring water to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, then add honey and lemon juice to taste.
Drink the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed.
tea_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis symptoms.
Shogaols: Shogaols can help suppress H. pylori growth, an bacteria commonly associated with gastritis, and improve overall gastric health.
Paradols: Paradols have gastroprotective effects, preventing damage to the stomach lining and promoting healing in gastritis.
Ginger Diarylheptanoids: These compounds can help inhibit gastric acid secretion, reducing acid reflux and associated gastritis symptoms.
Zerumbone: Zerumbone has antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining from oxidative stress and promote gastric health.

teas.tea_desc: Skullcap tea alleviates gastritis by relaxing smooth muscles in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing inflammation. This herbal tea's antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties also soothe the mucous membrane of the stomach, providing relief from gastritis symptoms. Moreover, skullcap tea stimulates the production of digestive juices, promoting healthy digestion and preventing the onset of gastritis. Finally, its demulcent qualities form a soothing protective film over the stomach lining, further alleviating discomfort caused by gastritis.
tea_parts: Leaves: Rich in flavonoids and antioxidants, leaves from the skullcap plant soothe inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis symptoms.
Flowers: Packed with tannins, flowers of the skullcap plant reduce stomach acidity, helping to heal gastritis.
Stems: Containing anti-inflammatory compounds, stems from the skullcap plant alleviate pain and discomfort caused by gastritis.
Rhyzomes: A powerhouse of antispasmodic properties, rhyzomes from the skullcap plant relax stomach muscles, providing comfort during gastritis flare-ups.
tea_recipe: In a cup, pour boiling water over 1-2 teaspoons of dried skullcap herb.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes to release the active compounds.
Strain the tea to remove the herb and ensure a smooth consistency.
Optional: Add honey or lemon to taste, but avoid milk or sugar.
Sip the skullcap tea slowly, allowing it to coat the stomach and soothe gastritis symptoms.
tea_constituents: Flavonoids: These antioxidants help reduce inflammation in the stomach lining, providing relief from gastritis.
Tannins: Astringent compounds that can help reduce stomach acid, protecting the stomach from irritation and damage caused by gastritis.
Phenolic acids: Offer anti-inflammatory and anti-spasmodic properties, which can alleviate pain and discomfort associated with gastritis.
Volatile oils: Contain antimicrobial properties, aiding in the prevention and treatment of H. pylori bacterial infections, a common cause of gastritis.
Saponins: Known to stimulate mucus secretion, which can help protect the stomach lining and promote healing of gastritis.
Bitter iridoids: Trigger digestive enzymes, aiding in proper digestion and reducing the risk of acid reflux, a contributing factor to gastritis.
